exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip).
instructions: Start standing upright with feet about shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip).
Brace your core, keep a neutral spine and chest lifted, and look forward throughout the movement.
Begin the descent by sending your hips back as if sitting into a chair, bending at the hips and knees simultaneously; keep weight distributed through your heels and mid-foot.
Lower until your thighs are roughly parallel to the floor (or slightly below if mobility allows) while ensuring your knees track over your toes and do not cave inward.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in your abs and legs.
Drive through your heels and mid-foot to extend the hips and knees, returning to standing while exhaling and keeping the dumbbells at your sides.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining controlled tempo; reduce range or weight if you cannot hold a neutral spine or if knees collapse inward.
exercises.description: A dumbbell squat is a standing, loaded lower-body squat performed while holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides; it primarily works the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes and posterior chain. It trains squat mechanics, lower-body strength, and core stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the platform and heels hanging off the back; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand tall (upright torso).
instructions: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the platform and heels hanging off the back; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand tall (upright torso).
Position feet about hip-width apart and keep a slight, natural bend in the knees (do not lock the knees).
Engage your abs to stabilize the torso and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Exhale and raise your heels by pressing through the balls of your feet until you are fully on your toes and calves are fully contracted; avoid rocking the torso forward.
Pause for 1–2 seconds at the top to maximize contraction.
Inhale and slowly lower your heels down below the step level until you feel a stretch in the calves (control the descent — do not drop abruptly).
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping movements smooth and maintaining balance; if needed, reduce weight or use a rail/wall for light support.
exercises.description: Standing calf raise performed on a raised step while holding dumbbells at the sides to load the lower leg; the movement raises the heels by plantarflexing the ankles to target the gastrocnemius/soleus (calves). Emphasis is on controlled concentric raise and slow eccentric lowering below the step for stretch.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Full Squat
exercises.notes: Stand upright (standing) under a loaded barbell set at about mid-chest height in a squat rack; step under the bar so it rests across your upper traps/shoulders (not on the neck).
instructions: Stand upright (standing) under a loaded barbell set at about mid-chest height in a squat rack; step under the bar so it rests across your upper traps/shoulders (not on the neck).
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width, lift the chest, pull the shoulder blades together, and unrack the bar by straightening the legs and stepping back to clear the rack.
Position feet shoulder-width to slightly wider with toes pointed slightly outward; establish a stable stance with weight distributed through the midfoot and heel.
Brace your core (abs) and keep a neutral spine; inhale before you descend to maintain intra-abdominal pressure.
Begin the descent by pushing the hips back and bending the knees simultaneously, keeping the chest up and spine neutral — descend until your hips drop below parallel (full squat depth).
Ensure knees track over the toes and do not collapse inward; keep the weight on the heels/midfoot and maintain even foot contact with the floor.
Pause briefly at the bottom if desired, then drive forcefully through the heels to extend the hips and knees and return to a fully upright position, exhaling as you ascend.
Control the movement on the way up and down; use safety pins or a spotter when training heavy and avoid rounding the lower back at any point.
exercises.description: A barbell back full squat performed to below-parallel depth; primarily loads the quadriceps while also recruiting the glutes, hamstrings and core for stabilization. The lifter descends with the bar on the upper back and then drives back up to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
Set a small, consistent bend in the knees (soft knees) — do not bend into a squat; this is a hinge at the hips, not a knee-dominant lift.
Take a breath in, brace your core, then push your hips back while keeping the chest up and spine neutral; allow the bar to travel down close to the front of your legs.
Lower the bar until you feel a strong but safe stretch in the hamstrings (typically to mid‑shin), keeping the bar close to the body and shoulders pulled back — avoid rounding the lower back.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the hamstrings and back; keep your head in a neutral alignment (looking a few feet in front of you).
Exhale and drive your hips forward by contracting the glutes and hamstrings to return to the standing start position; do not hyperextend the lower back at the top.
Control the descent and ascent on every rep, using a weight that lets you maintain a neutral spine and full hip hinge pattern.
If grip is limiting, switch to a mixed grip or use straps; always prioritize form over load and stop if you feel lumbar rounding or sharp pain.
exercises.description: A bilateral hip-hinge performed from standing that emphasizes the hamstrings by lowering a loaded barbell along the thighs with a neutral spine and slight knee bend; returned to standing by driving the hips forward and squeezing the glutes. Builds posterior chain strength and improves hip extension.

exercises.name: Barbell Mixed grip Deadlift
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower your torso until you can grip the bar; use a mixed grip (one palm facing you, one palm facing away) with hands slightly outside the knees.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart with the bar over the mid-foot while standing (upright position to start).
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower your torso until you can grip the bar; use a mixed grip (one palm facing you, one palm facing away) with hands slightly outside the knees.
Set your back: pull the shoulder blades back, lift the chest, and maintain a neutral spine (avoid rounding) while your hips sit between a low and mid position.
Brace your core and take a full breath, keep the bar close to your shins (bar touches or tracks just above the mid-foot).
Drive through the heels and extend the hips and knees simultaneously to lift the bar in a controlled hip-hinge, keeping the bar close to your body and shoulders over or slightly in front of the bar.
Stand tall at the top by fully extending the hips and squeezing the glutes; avoid excessive lumbar extension (no hyperextension of the spine).
Lower the bar under control by hinging at the hips first, then bending the knees when the bar passes them, returning the bar to the floor while maintaining a neutral spine (repeat for desired reps).
If using heavy loads, alternate which hand is supinated between sets to balance grip stress and reduce muscle imbalances.
exercises.description: A conventional barbell deadlift using a mixed (over/under) grip to improve grip security while lifting; it trains the hip hinge to drive the bar from the floor to standing, emphasizing the posterior chain. The mixed grip helps prevent the bar from rolling in heavy lifts.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and foot platform so your knees are roughly at 90 degrees when your feet are on the plate and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the seat and foot platform so your knees are roughly at 90 degrees when your feet are on the plate and select an appropriate weight.
Sit with your back fully supported against the pad and both feet placed hip-width on the lower edge of the foot platform (balls of feet and toes on the platform, heels free to move).
Grip the handles or seat sides for stability and ensure the safety latch or stops are set before starting.
Unlatch the machine (if applicable) and begin from a neutral/dorsiflexed position with a slight stretch in the calves.
Exhale and press through the balls of your feet by plantarflexing the ankles to raise the platform until your calves are fully contracted; do not use the knees or hips to drive the movement.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the calves, then inhale and slowly lower the platform under control until you feel a stretch in the calves (avoid dropping quickly or bouncing).
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then re-engage the safety latch before exiting the machine.
exercises.description: Seated on a lever calf press machine, this exercise uses controlled ankle plantarflexion to raise the foot platform and load the calf muscles. It emphasizes the gastrocnemius/soleus complex while the torso and knees remain supported.

exercises.name: Lever Leg Extension (Inward Toes)
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the leg extension machine with your back firmly against the back pad and adjust the seat so the machine's knee pivot lines up with your knee joint.
instructions: Sit upright on the leg extension machine with your back firmly against the back pad and adjust the seat so the machine's knee pivot lines up with your knee joint.
Position the lower pad just above your ankles and place your feet on the footpad with your toes turned inward (feet slightly internally rotated). Grip the side handles or seat for stability.
Start with knees bent at about 90 degrees (or the machine's start position). Brace your core and keep your spine neutral; do not arch your lower back.
Exhale and extend your knees, pushing the pad upward until your legs are nearly straight but avoid locking the knees at full extension.
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the quads, then inhale and lower the weight slowly and with control back to the start position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining the inward toe position and steady tempo throughout. Stop if you feel sharp knee pain and ensure the movement is smooth and controlled.
exercises.description: Seated leg extension performed on a lever/machine with the toes turned inward; the movement isolates knee extension to target the quadriceps while the inward toe position slightly alters quad activation. The torso stays seated against the back pad and the lower shins press into the machine pad as you extend and lower the legs under control.

exercises.name: Lever Sitting Toe Raise
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the footplate with your heels hanging off the edge and grasp the machine handles or sides of the bench for stability.
instructions: Sit on the machine bench with your knees bent at about 90 degrees and the padded lever resting across your lower thighs just above the knees.
Place the balls of your feet on the footplate with your heels hanging off the edge and grasp the machine handles or sides of the bench for stability.
Start with your heels lowered to feel a stretch in the calves (ankles dorsiflexed).
Exhale and press down through the balls of your feet, plantarflexing the ankles to raise your heels as high as possible while keeping the knees stationary.
Hold the top position for a brief pause, squeezing the calves, then slowly lower your heels back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping a controlled tempo and maintaining upright torso and stable knee position throughout.
exercises.description: A seated calf-raise performed on a lever-style machine that isolates the lower leg by plantarflexing the ankle against resistance while the knees are bent. It emphasizes the soleus and gastrocnemius to build calf strength and size.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Deadlift
exercises.notes: Approach the bar by hinging at the hips and bend the knees until your shins touch the bar; keep hips higher than a squat but lower than full upright.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart with the barbell over the mid-foot and toes pointing slightly out.
Approach the bar by hinging at the hips and bend the knees until your shins touch the bar; keep hips higher than a squat but lower than full upright.
Grip the bar just outside your knees with a double overhand or mixed grip; set shoulders slightly in front of the bar and keep the bar close to your shins.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, keep a neutral (flat) spine and chest lifted — avoid rounding the back.
Drive through the heels and simultaneously extend hips and knees, keeping the bar close to your body and the torso angle changing minimally at first.
Stand tall at the top by finishing hip extension and squeezing the glutes; do not hyperextend the lower back — shoulders should be back but not excessively retracted.
To descend, hinge at the hips first by pushing them back, then bend the knees once the bar passes them, lowering the bar under control to the floor while maintaining a neutral spine.
Reset your breath and core brace at the bottom for the next repetition; maintain consistent foot placement, bar path, and posture throughout the set.
exercises.description: A hip-hinge strength exercise that lifts a loaded barbell from the floor by extending the hips and knees; it targets the posterior chain with heavy involvement of the hamstrings and glutes while the back and core stabilize the torso.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Leg Diagonal Kickback
exercises.notes: Attach an ankle cuff to a low cable pulley and secure it around the working ankle.
instructions: Attach an ankle cuff to a low cable pulley and secure it around the working ankle.

Stand upright facing the machine with the non-working foot planted on the floor (or on a small balance pad) and a slight bend in that knee; grasp the machine or hold a handle with one hand for light support and stabilization.
Shift your weight onto the planted leg, brace your abs to keep the torso stable and upright, and allow the working leg to hang straight behind you with toes pointed or slightly flexed.

Without bending the working knee, drive the leg straight back and diagonally across your body (slightly outward and behind), contracting the glute at the top of the movement; avoid arching the lower back or leaning the torso forward.
Pause briefly at full extension while squeezing the glute, then slowly return the leg to the starting position under control without letting the cable pull your torso out of alignment.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides and repeat.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-leg diagonal kickbacks use a low-pulley ankle attachment to extend a straight working leg backward and slightly across the midline, emphasizing hip extension and glute contraction while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.name: Barbell Hang Clean
exercises.notes: Hinge slightly at the hips and bend the knees to set a strong athletic posture: neutral spine, chest up, eyes forward and core braced.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell held at thigh/upper-thigh level (hang position) using a slightly wider-than-shoulder grip, wrists straight and elbows pointed down.
Hinge slightly at the hips and bend the knees to set a strong athletic posture: neutral spine, chest up, eyes forward and core braced.
Initiate the move by forcefully extending the hips and knees (triple extension) and pushing through the midfoot and forefoot to generate upward momentum on the bar.
As the bar rises, shrug the shoulders aggressively and pull the elbows high and out to accelerate the bar close to the body; keep the bar path vertical and close.

Explosively pull yourself under the bar by quickly bending the knees and rotating the elbows under so the bar is received on the front of the shoulders (front-rack) with the elbows high and upper arms roughly parallel to the floor.

Catch the bar in a partial squat or quarter squat (depending on load and intent) with an upright torso, controlled breath, and firm grip; ensure wrist and elbow position supports the bar on the shoulders.
Stand up fully from the catch by extending hips and knees to finish the repetition with hips locked out and shoulders stacked over hips.
Lower the bar in a controlled manner back to the hang position (upper-thigh), reset posture and breathing, then repeat for the desired reps.
Use lighter loads to practice timing and technique before increasing weight; keep the spine neutral throughout and avoid pulling with the arms only.
exercises.description: The barbell hang clean is a power, compound lift performed from a standing hip/upper-thigh position that uses an explosive hip and knee extension to drive the bar upward, followed by pulling yourself under the bar and catching it in a front-rack position. It trains lower-body power, posterior chain drive, upper-back shrugging and a quick catch in the front of the shoulders.

exercises.name: Smith Staggered Stance Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Set the Smith bar at hip level and position safety stops a few inches below the lowest intended range.
instructions: Set the Smith bar at hip level and position safety stops a few inches below the lowest intended range.
Stand inside the Smith rack in a staggered stance (one foot forward, one foot back) with the bar held with an overhand grip at arm's length; feet should be hip-width apart side-to-side.
Slightly bend both knees (soft knee) and brace your core, retracting the shoulder blades so the upper back stays tight and flat.

Hinge at the hips by pushing your hips straight back while keeping the bar close to the legs and maintaining a neutral spine; lower the bar until you feel a stretch in the hamstrings or the bar reaches just below the knees.
Keep the weight distributed through the heel of the front foot and the midfoot of the back foot; do not let the lower back round.
Reverse the movement by driving the hips forward and contracting the glutes and hamstrings to return to the start position while keeping the upper back engaged.
Repeat for the desired reps, then re-rack the bar safely and switch which foot is forward to train both legs.
exercises.description: A Romanian deadlift performed in a staggered stance on a Smith machine; you hinge at the hips with one foot slightly forward to emphasize the hamstrings while the fixed bar provides a controlled path. The movement loads the posterior chain while requiring a neutral spine and upper-back bracing.

exercises.name: Lever Leg Extension (Inward Toes)
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the leg extension machine with your back firmly against the back pad and adjust the seat so the machine's knee pivot lines up with your knee joint.
instructions: Sit upright on the leg extension machine with your back firmly against the back pad and adjust the seat so the machine's knee pivot lines up with your knee joint.
Position the lower pad just above your ankles and place your feet on the footpad with your toes turned inward (feet slightly internally rotated). Grip the side handles or seat for stability.
Start with knees bent at about 90 degrees (or the machine's start position). Brace your core and keep your spine neutral; do not arch your lower back.
Exhale and extend your knees, pushing the pad upward until your legs are nearly straight but avoid locking the knees at full extension.
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the quads, then inhale and lower the weight slowly and with control back to the start position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining the inward toe position and steady tempo throughout. Stop if you feel sharp knee pain and ensure the movement is smooth and controlled.
exercises.description: Seated leg extension performed on a lever/machine with the toes turned inward; the movement isolates knee extension to target the quadriceps while the inward toe position slightly alters quad activation. The torso stays seated against the back pad and the lower shins press into the machine pad as you extend and lower the legs under control.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Single Leg Extension
exercises.notes: Attach an ankle cuff to a low pulley on a cable machine and fasten it securely around one ankle.
instructions: Attach an ankle cuff to a low pulley on a cable machine and fasten it securely around one ankle.
Sit upright on a bench (slightly inclined or upright) facing away from the machine so the cable pulls across the front of the knee; brace your torso and place hands on the bench or pad for support.
Start with the working knee bent ~90 degrees and foot relaxed, keeping the thigh pressed against the seat.
Exhale and extend the knee by contracting the quad until the leg is nearly straight (avoid fully locking the knee).
Hold the top position briefly with controlled tension in the quad.
Inhale and slowly lower the foot back to the start position, resisting the cable for a controlled eccentric phase.
Complete the desired reps, then switch legs and repeat; maintain an upright seated posture and keep hips stationary throughout.
exercises.description: Seated single-leg cable extension isolates knee extension to work one quadriceps at a time using a low-pulley cable and ankle cuff. It emphasizes controlled concentric extension and slow eccentric return for quad strength and balance.

exercises.name: Smith Seated Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Setup the Smith machine: place a flat bench in the rack so you can sit with feet on a raised block or plate with heels hanging off the edge; set the bar height so it rests across your upper thighs/knees when seated.
instructions: Setup the Smith machine: place a flat bench in the rack so you can sit with feet on a raised block or plate with heels hanging off the edge; set the bar height so it rests across your upper thighs/knees when seated.

Sit on the bench with knees bent about 90 degrees, shins vertical, feet hip-width apart and toes pointing forward on the block so only the balls of the feet contact the platform; place the Smith barpad or a pad across your upper thighs just above the knees and unhook the bar.
Brace your core, keep an upright seated posture (not slouched), and grip the bar lightly for stability; ensure your knees remain bent throughout the movement (seated position).
Lower your heels slowly by dorsiflexing the ankles until you feel a stretch in the calves (heels drop below the level of the balls of the feet), keeping control and no bouncing.
Drive through the balls of the feet and contract your calves to raise the heels as high as possible (plantarflexion), hold a brief peak contraction at the top.
Lower back down under control to the starting stretched position and repeat for the desired number of reps. Re-rack the bar safely when finished.
Notes: use a controlled tempo, avoid using knee extension or hip drive to lift the weight, and adjust foot position (toes in/out) if you want to emphasize different parts of the calf.
exercises.description: Seated calf raise performed in a Smith machine; sitting with knees bent under the bar, you raise and lower the heels to work the calf muscles through full ankle plantarflexion and dorsiflexion. This variation emphasizes the soleus portion of the calves due to the knee-flexed position.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
